mishap.hexboard.bad_operation: Execute board operation failed, caused by: %s, %s
page.hexboard.board_operation.0: These five patterns are added by HexBoard. $(br)Common rule: argument #1 is always a $(bold)vector$() pointing to any block in the target connected board.
page.hexboard.board_operation.load: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = start slot index, $(br)3) any/list = iota source. $(br)Load writes from the start index; if arg #3 is not a List Iota, it is treated as a single-element list.
page.hexboard.board_operation.delete: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index. $(br)Delete removes all slots in [l, r]; reversed bounds are normalized automatically.
page.hexboard.board_operation.move: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index, $(br)4) number = destination start index. $(br)Move relocates [left, right] to target if the destination range is legal.
page.hexboard.board_operation.resolve: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index. $(br)Resolve evaluates the selected range and returns one List Iota containing the result sequence.
page.hexboard.board_operation.literal_re: Args: $(br)1) vector = target board position, $(br)2) number = left index, $(br)3) number = right index. $(br)Literal Resolve uses literal behavior on the same range and also returns one List Iota.
page.hexboard.board.0: I really hope to facilitate the creation of large-scale spells, as using just the staff and those few actions to edit patterns is truly headache-inducing. But now it's different—I found a spell editing system called "Board" in a near-age mechanic's manuscript, improved upon it, and built a working prototype. First, we need to craft the board itself. Place the Board block on the ground; the side with basalt and calcite is the front, and the edified wood side is the back.
page.hexboard.board.1: Boards with the same orientation that are connected are treated as a single unit in subsequent operations. For convenience, "Board" hereafter basically refers to this unified whole.
page.hexboard.board.2: A board has many slots for placing "Typeblocks" on its front face, much like ancient movable type printing—we compose spells by arranging different typeblocks on the board. Each slot on the board is indexed in sequence; if you look at the board while wearing lens, you'll see the index of the currently targeted slot. $(br)Now comes the main event—"Typeblocks." We all know that a spell is essentially a list Iota; by using typeblocks to correspond to elements in this list Iota, we can conveniently compose spells.
page.hexboard.board.3: First is the most basic "Quartz Typeblock," which resembles a slate and can have a pattern Iota inscribed on it; when placed on board, it represents the pattern inscribed above. Oh, I forgot to mention—right-clicking the board while holding any typeblock will place that typeblock into the corresponding slot. $(br)Then there's the "Wool Typeblock," used to represent an Iota position that follows an Evaluation in a spell; a wool typeblock can hold any type of Iota. But note that, like thought knots, it's a one-time write and cannot be modified. Additionally, there are two special typeblocks that I'll leave for later.
page.hexboard.board.4: Having just the aforementioned content isn't enough for convenient editing. To address this, I crafted an operational tool called the "Board Staff." It can store an Iota (like a Focus) or operate the Board. The board staff has many different operation modes; hold the board staff in your hand and sneak while scrolling the mouse wheel to change modes, or bind shortcuts for modes in the settings.
page.hexboard.board.5: Let's introduce each of board staff's operation modes. $(br)$(bold)1. Add Mode$(): When in this mode, right-clicking or left-clicking a board slot opens a hexagonal grid similar to the staff spell-casting interface, where you can draw patterns. After drawing a pattern, the board staff attempts to place a quartz typeblock inscribed with that pattern into the slot. Specifically, it first searches the operator's inventory for a quartz typeblock already inscribed with that pattern; if found, it uses it. If not found, it searches again for any quartz typeblock, inscribes the corresponding pattern, and places it. If still not found, the operation fails and nothing happens.
page.hexboard.board.6: $(bold)2. Delete Mode$(): When in this mode, right-clicking a board slot allows you to select a range of adjacent slots by index (referred to as a "Selection"). Specifically, right-click the left endpoint slot and right endpoint slot of the desired range. Sneak right-click resets the current selection. Left-click or sneak left-click, if there's a selection, removes all typeblocks within the selection and places them into the operator's inventory; if there's no selection, it removes the typeblock from the currently targeted slot. Notably, this pattern of "right-click to select, sneak right-click to reset selection, left-click to execute" is common to many subsequent operation modes.
page.hexboard.board.7: $(bold)3. Move Mode$(): When in this mode, the board staff can right-click to select a range, sneak right-click to reset the selection, and left-click attempts to move the selection to the current target position; of course, if there's a conflict, nothing happens. Additionally, during operation, a transparent color overlay appears on the board to indicate the operation. $(br)$(bold)4. Copy Mode$(): Similar to move mode, but copies the selection instead of moving it; obtaining typeblocks from the inventory works the same as in add mode.
page.hexboard.board.8: $(bold)5. Resolve Mode$(): Right-click to select a range, sneak right-click to reset the selection, left-click attempts to resolve the selection. Specifically, this combines all Iotas represented by Typeblocks in the Selection into a List Iota in index order. Then it checks whether the operator is holding an Iota container capable of storing this List Iota (such as a Focus or Board Staff), and stores the List Iota in that container. $(br)$(bold)6. Literal Resolve Mode$(): Similar to Resolve Mode, but performs "Literal Resolve." For Quartz and Wool Typeblocks, the Iota represented during Literal Resolve and Resolve is the same. However, this differs for the two special Typeblocks, which I'll leave for later.
page.hexboard.board.9: $(bold)7. Load Mode$(): Left-click attempts to read an Iota from the Iota container in hand, resolve it, and place Typeblocks starting from the current target slot. Specifically, if a List Iota is read, its elements are processed in order: Pattern Iotas are placed as corresponding Quartz Typeblocks, and other general Iotas are placed as corresponding Wool Typeblocks. Additionally, two special Iotas are placed as the two special Typeblocks. If what's read is not a List Iota, it's treated as "a List Iota containing only that element." Obtaining Typeblocks from the inventory works the same as in Add Mode.
page.hexboard.board.10: With the aforementioned content, composing spells is already quite effective, but I've also created some useful additions. If you look at a Quartz Typeblock through a Lens, and the Pattern on it corresponds to an Operation, the name of that Operation will be displayed. If you're carrying a Hex Notebook and that Pattern is recorded in the Hex Notebook, a shortcut key to that record will be displayed, though sometimes recognition may not be entirely accurate.
page.hexboard.board.11: If you open the notebook to a page with a Pattern drawn on it, pressing Ctrl, Alt, or Shift and left-clicking the Pattern will attempt to place a Quartz Typeblock inscribed with that Pattern into the currently targeted slot. If a page has two Patterns, left-click corresponds to the left one and right-click to the right one, such as this page.
page.hexboard.board.12: Finally, let me explain the two special Typeblocks I hadn't mentioned before, which I call the "Macro" system. The first is the $(bold)Lapis Typeblock$(), which serves as a "Macro Definition." Specifically, you can inscribe a Pattern Iota onto a Lapis Typeblock, but when read out, it becomes a non-Pattern Iota called a "Macro Definition Pattern" that looks the same as the original Pattern. When placed on the Board, the Lapis Typeblock forms a "Macro" from a sequence of Typeblocks connected to its right (higher index). The second is the $(bold)Redstone Typeblock$(), which serves as a "Macro Call." Specifically, similar to the Lapis Typeblock, but the inscribed Pattern becomes the corresponding "Macro Call Pattern."
page.hexboard.board.13: When resolving reaches a Redstone Typeblock, it jumps to the Lapis Typeblock on the same Board with the same Pattern and resolves the Macro following it; this doesn't require the Lapis Typeblock to be included in the Selection (and it shouldn't be). However, when performing Literal Resolve, a Redstone Typeblock only resolves to its Macro Call Pattern Iota, and a Lapis Typeblock only resolves to its corresponding Macro Definition Pattern. $(br)Don't use recursive Macros! Because that will result in call-call-call-call... until consciousness fades.
page.hexboard.board.14: Ah, finally finished writing. Let me complain about that mechanic here. In that mechanic's manuscript, there was no design for a board staff; he planned to control the Board's operation modes via redstone and operate by directly clicking with bare hands. There was no transparent color overlay for assistance, nor a Macro system. I must say, the progress of hexcasting in recent years has been truly tremendous. $(br)Addendum: I've also created some $(l:hexcasting:patterns/board_operation)patterns that can directly operate the board.$()
